# Generated bytecode cache wrapper

`GeneratedBytecodeCacheWrapper` models the preferred interception seam for Janino-style source compilation:

```text
generateBytecodes(String requestedClassName) -> Map<String, byte[]>
```

The wrapper consumes or stores the complete generated class map from one exact compilation request. Individual `defineClass` calls remain outside the cache layer.

## Hit behavior

An exact SPJB hit returns a new mutable `LinkedHashMap` containing defensive class-byte copies. The original generator is not called. Class definition, protection domains, linkage, and loader behavior remain the responsibility of the original caller.

## Fallback behavior

A missing, corrupt, unreadable, invalid, or unwritable cache always executes the original generator exactly once.

- The original returned map object is passed through unchanged.
- A `null` original result is passed through unchanged and is not cached.
- Original checked exceptions retain their type and object identity.
- Invalid complete maps are not cached.
- Cache write failures become diagnostic wrapper results and do not escape into the caller.

Only `ThreadDeath` and `VirtualMachineError` cross cache containment boundaries.

## Exact context

Every lookup uses `GeneratedBytecodeContext`, which requires exact hashes for:

- Starsector build
- Janino implementation
- ordered classpath
- complete ordered source/dependency graph
- compiler options
- parent classloader identity
- protection-domain policy

A live adapter may add stricter context inputs and may never omit these components. The real Starsector Janino target remains blocked until installation signatures and dependency lookup behavior are reviewed.

## SPJB read safety

SPJB reads reject symbolic links and non-file targets. File reads are bounded during streaming so concurrent growth cannot escape the file-size ceiling.

## Synthetic proof

The synthetic worker compiles one source request into:

- a top-level class
- a named inner class
- an anonymous class

It defines and executes the returned classes through a fresh classloader. Tests launch separate JVMs for cold generation, warm cache consumption, and corrupt-cache repair, and compare exact complete-map hashes.

This proves persistent complete-map reuse on a synthetic target. It makes no real-install speed claim.
